\boxed{B}
**Clinical Reasoning:** Midostaurin is specifically described as an FLT3 inhibitor used in FLT3-mutated acute myeloid leukemia; none of the cited sources indicate any effect on angiogenesis or inhibition of pathways like VEGF/VEGFR, FGFR, PDGFR, or others implicated in neovascularization. While some TKIs (e.g., nintedanib) do inhibit angiogenesis via multi-targeted tyrosine kinase suppression, midostaurin lacks documented anti-angiogenic properties in these references. Therefore, based solely on the provided literature, midostaurin does not inhibit angiogenesis.
